artifacts.content:
From: jonathan.reeves84@gmail.com
To: emma.reeves@somewhere.com
Subject: Weekend woodturning logistics + general life admin
Date: 5 February 2025

Hey Em,

On the bus back from Edinburgh Park so using the dead time to brain-dump about March and everything else.

For the Chippendale woodturning weekend (15–16 March), I’m still planning to drive from here both days. I’ll leave Bruntsfield around 7:45am so I’m not stressing about traffic on the A1. Grey Golf, Google Maps, same as when we go out towards North Berwick. I’d rather commute than pay for a B&B when the mortgage already feels tight – that fixed rate we locked in back in 2020 is fine, but there isn’t a lot of slack, and I keep fiddling with spreadsheets in case my income drops 20–30% if I end up taking a lower‑paid role or going part‑time again to avoid another burnout.

I know you’d prefer me to switch off from work, but with SkyeBridge muttering about a 2025 restructure and my manager hinting in January that senior engineers at my band might get offered voluntary redundancy, it’s hard not to scenario‑plan. I’m weirdly calm about it, maybe because I’ve secretly been sketching that five‑year idea of doing part‑time remote coding plus a few paid woodworking commissions. Nothing dramatic – just the “Bruntsfield Reeves” scribbles in my notebook when you and Sophie are asleep.

Re health stuff: I’ll pack the Ventolin and my FFP3 mask. I still think about that stupid evening in late 2023 in the basement when I sanded without the mask, wheezed myself into a mild asthma attack, and you ended up driving me to A&E at the Royal Infirmary. My GP at Bruntsfield Medical Practice keeps going on about “occupational dust exposure”, so I’ll be sensible about extraction and breaks.

Speaking of breaks, my back was grumbling again this morning. Your colleague at the Tollcross clinic is a miracle worker but I don’t love that the pain is now sometimes going down my left leg. When she said it might be the start of a disc issue, I had a little internal panic. I’ll make sure to move around at the lathe and not stand frozen like I do at my desk.

We can sit down this weekend and rejig nursery drop‑offs for that course weekend, plus maybe talk (again) about the whole second‑child question. I know the thought of another baby while we’re also gearing up to support Mum and Dad with downsizing is a lot.

Bus is nearly at Bruntsfield Links. See you in a bit.

J x

metadata.subject: Weekend woodturning logistics + general life admin

artifacts.content:
From: jonathan.reeves84@gmail.com
To: daniel.reeves@gmail.com
Subject: Quick update before you disappear to Glasgow again
Date: 10 February 2025

Hey Dan,

Realised we’ve mostly been trading memes on WhatsApp and not actually talking, so here’s a proper update while Sophie is attempting to feed porridge to her toy rabbit.

Work first: things at SkyeBridge are… fine-ish. Official line is “possible restructure later this year”. Unofficially, my manager let slip in our January one‑to‑one that I’m on some kind of key‑person watch list because of the legacy reporting stuff I know, and that they’re floating a voluntary redundancy package for people at my level. Not exactly reassuring when you’ve got a tight five‑year fixed mortgage and a three‑year‑old.

On the plus side, the woodturning course in March at the Chippendale school is keeping me sane. You’d laugh if you saw my search history from late 2024 – I went down a huge rabbit hole on Reddit reading reviews of the place and watching their YouTube workshop tours during “quiet” afternoons in the Edinburgh Park office. I keep worrying that if anyone ever pulled my browsing logs they’d think I’d checked out of software entirely.

You know how burnt out I was in London; since then I’ve had these on‑and‑off patches of low mood and anxiety. Nothing dramatic, just that 2am brain‑spin. Planning this weekend surrounded by lathes and wood shavings has weirdly become a coping mechanism. I’ll be driving down from Bruntsfield both days – leaving about 7:45am to get along the A1 – rather than staying nearby. Partly money, partly wanting to be back to help with bedtime.

Health‑wise I’m mostly okay. Still kicking myself about that late‑2023 basement incident when I sanded without a mask and ended up in A&E at the Royal Infirmary wheezing like an idiot. My GP at Bruntsfield Medical Practice now has “occupational dust exposure” in my notes, which makes me sound like a Victorian miner. And I’m seeing one of Emma’s colleagues at the Tollcross physio clinic monthly for this lower‑back thing that sometimes sends a twinge down my left leg. She muttered about a possible disc issue, which is not what you want to hear when you’re fantasising about more time on your feet at a workbench.

How are you and Laura getting on? Any more thoughts on kids, or are you still enjoying the lie‑ins too much? We’re still in the “do we really want to roll the dice on a second one while also preparing to help Mum and Dad downsize?” phase.

Let’s try for a Stirling meet‑up in March – maybe the weekend after my course.

J

metadata.subject: Quick update before you disappear to Glasgow again

artifacts.content:
Title: Late-night woodworking / five-year plan brain dump
Date: 18 January 2025
Location: Living room table

Everyone finally asleep. Kettle on, notebook out.

Why this matters:

Since the London burnout years, I’ve had these recurring waves of low mood and anxiety – nothing formally diagnosed, but enough that I lie awake at 1–2am doing worst‑case spreadsheets. Weirdly, watching Chippendale School YouTube tours and planning the March 2025 woodturning weekend has been one of the few things that reliably calms me down. It feels like progress instead of just doom‑scrolling.

Five‑year sketch (2025–2030):

1. Work
- Stay at SkyeBridge for now, but keep in mind the hinted 2025 restructure and possible voluntary redundancy for senior engineers. If a decent package appears, that could be the bridge into a different balance.
- Accept that a 20–30% income drop is plausible if I switch to part‑time/remote or a less stressful role. Mortgage on the Bruntsfield flat is tight but survivable if we trim extras (no random accommodation for courses, drive from home instead, etc.).

2. Woodworking
- Use the March woodturning weekend as a test: can my back and asthma cope with two full days at the lathe?
  - Back: monthly physio at Emma’s Tollcross clinic is helping, but the fact that the pain sometimes radiates down my left leg and they’ve mentioned a possible disc issue is a flag. Need to build in movement, not freeze.
  - Asthma: never again repeat the late‑2023 basement sanding stupidity that landed me in A&E at the Royal Infirmary wheezing. Always mask (FFP3), extraction if possible, Ventolin in pocket. GP at Bruntsfield Medical Practice is already twitchy about “occupational dust exposure”.
- Start a small portfolio of pieces for friends/family. Quietly think about a name: “Bruntsfield Reeves”, “Reeves & Bruntsfield”, etc. No public commitments until I’m sure this isn’t just a midlife fantasy.

3. Family
- Keep talking with Emma about second‑child question. She said last summer she’s “not sure I can handle another baby if we’re also supporting your mum and dad financially.” Fair. With Dad’s recent memory lapses (including getting lost on the way to the Co‑op in North Berwick) and the looming downsizing, we’re heading into a caregiving phase whether we like it or not.

For now: breathe, sleep, turn some wood in March, reassess after.

metadata.title: Late-night woodworking / five-year plan brain dump

artifacts.content:
From: jonathan.reeves84@gmail.com
To: margaret.reeves@btinternet.com
Subject: This weekend + a wee favour
Date: 2 March 2025

Hi Mum,

Hope you and Dad are keeping warm – Sophie keeps asking when she can go back to the beach, so I’ve promised her the first vaguely sunny Saturday in North Berwick is yours.

I wanted to check whether next Sunday works for me to come through and help with the paperwork sorting you mentioned. I know you’re only “thinking aloud” about moving, but if we can at least get the files in some kind of order it’ll make life easier later.

You said on the phone that Dad’s been a bit more forgetful. I’m still thinking about what you told me – him getting disoriented on the way to the Co‑op on High Street the other week. I don’t want to make a drama out of it, but it does make me feel better knowing you’ve asked the GP to keep an eye on things. I can take a look at any letters when I’m there.

One reason I’m keen not to stay away overnight for the woodturning weekend mid‑month is exactly this juggle between you two, Sophie, and work. The course is at the Chippendale school out near Haddington on 15–16 March, so I’ll just drive from our flat in Bruntsfield both days – leave about 7:45am, head down the A1, and be back in time for tea. It also saves on accommodation costs, which is no bad thing when the mortgage already feels a bit tight.

Health‑wise I’m fine, so please don’t worry. I’ve got the asthma under control as long as I use a proper mask and don’t repeat the daftness from late 2023 when I sanded in our basement without one and ended up at A&E at the Royal Infirmary. My GP at Bruntsfield Medical Practice gave me a mild telling‑off and now bangs on about “occupational dust exposure”, but as long as I keep my Ventolin handy it’s manageable.

My back is still a bit stiff from all the desk time, and I’ve been seeing one of Emma’s colleagues at the Tollcross clinic once a month. She thinks it might be the start of a disc issue because the ache sometimes goes down my left leg, so I just have to remember to move around rather than stand bolt upright at the lathe.

Let me know about Sunday, and give Dad a hug from me. We’ll bring Sophie through soon for a beach walk if the wind behaves.

Love,

Jon

Title: Woodturning weekend – Chippendale School
Date: 15–16 March 2025
Location: Chippendale International School of Furniture, near Haddington

All‑day event (both days)

Notes/logistics:

- Travel: Drive from home in Bruntsfield both days. Leave flat around 7:45am to allow for traffic on A702/A1. Grey 2017 VW Golf, Google Maps sat‑nav to the school. Tank up on Friday night.
- Backup: If Golf ends up in the garage, take train from Edinburgh Waverley to Drem, then taxi from Drem station to the school.
- Money: No local accommodation – commute to avoid extra costs while mortgage is tight and in case income drops 20–30% if I end up taking a lower‑paid/part‑time role post‑restructure at SkyeBridge.

Family cover:

- Emma doing nursery drop‑off both days; I’ll aim to be back in Edinburgh by 5:30–6pm for dinner/bedtime.
- Keep phone on vibrate in pocket – Emma is emergency contact if anything happens. We’re still mid‑discussion about second baby vs. helping Mum and Dad financially with downsizing from North Berwick, so being around in the evenings matters.

Health reminders:

- Asthma: Bring Ventolin inhaler (jacket pocket). Wear FFP3 mask any time there’s fine dust. Remember that late‑2023 basement sanding session that ended in a mild asthma attack and an unplanned A&E trip to the Royal Infirmary – no repeat performances. GP at Bruntsfield Medical Practice already has “occupational dust exposure” in notes.
- Back: Do physio stretches in the morning and at lunch. Don’t stand locked at the lathe; move feet, walk around every 30–40 mins. Lower back pain still occasionally radiates into left leg; Tollcross physio suspects early disc issue, so be sensible.

Headspace:

- Enjoy it. This weekend is partly a sanity break after London burnout, the low‑mood patches, and current SkyeBridge redundancy rumours.
- Pay attention to whether full days on the tools feel sustainable for future “Bruntsfield Reeves” type side‑projects.

metadata.title: Woodturning weekend – Chippendale School
metadata.location: Chippendale International School of Furniture, near Haddington

artifacts.content:
Thread: Jonathan & Emma – late night chat
Date: 28 August 2024

Emma: You still up? Thought you were coming to bed an hour ago.

Jonathan: Sorry, went down a woodworking rabbit hole again. Watching that Chippendale School YouTube tour for the 100th time.

Emma: Is this the “coping mechanism” night? You’ve got that post‑London burnout look.

Jonathan: Guilty. The low‑mood/anxiety thing is back a bit. Planning a weekend with lathes feels better than staring at the ceiling doing mortgage maths.

Emma: Speaking of… you were talking earlier about maybe dropping to 4 days if SkyeBridge gets more intense.

Jonathan: Yeah. I keep running spreadsheets assuming a 20–30% pay cut. With the Bruntsfield flat mortgage already tight, it’s not pretty, but not impossible. Just means no fancy holidays and definitely no paying for accommodation on top of course fees – I’d rather drive out and back.

Emma: And the whole “Bruntsfield Reeves” side‑project idea?

Jonathan: Still just scribbles in the notebook. But if I ever take one of those voluntary redundancy packages they’re whispering about for 2025, it could be the nudge.

Emma: One crisis at a time. We still haven’t settled the baby question.

Jonathan: I know. When you said, “I’m not sure I can handle another baby if we’re also supporting your mum and dad financially,” it kind of lodged in my brain.

Emma: Well, look at your dad. He got lost on the way to the Co‑op in North Berwick last month. Your mum’s clearly worried, even if she won’t say “dementia” out loud yet.

Jonathan: Yeah. Helping them downsize is going to take time and money.

Emma: And you’ve got your own body to think about – the asthma, your back.

Jonathan: True. I still feel like an idiot about that basement sanding session last year that landed me in A&E at the Royal Infirmary. And now the Tollcross physio is muttering about a possible disc issue because the pain goes down my left leg.

Emma: So: look after yourself, keep watching your nerdy wood videos, and come to bed.

Jonathan: Coming. Thanks.

From: jonathan.reeves84@gmail.com
To: laura.mckay@gmail.com
Subject: Quick favour re backs + random life update
Date: 12 April 2025

Hi Laura,

Hope Glasgow is treating you both well and that Daniel hasn’t roped you into too many DIY disasters.

I wanted to pick your brain professionally for a second. You know I’ve had on‑and‑off lower back stiffness from all the years at a desk. Since early last year it’s been worse – ache across the lower back, sometimes a line of pain down my left leg. I’ve been seeing one of Emma’s colleagues at the Tollcross clinic once a month, and she’s hinted it might be the start of a disc issue. Nothing dramatic yet, but enough that I’m trying to be more sensible about how long I stand at the workbench/lathe without moving.

I did the weekend woodturning course at the Chippendale school in March and loved it, but two full days on my feet definitely lit things up. Combine that with the mild asthma (and my stupidity in late 2023 when I sanded in our basement without a mask, had a flare‑up, and ended up in A&E at the Royal Infirmary), and I’m very aware my body isn’t 21 anymore. My GP at Bruntsfield Medical Practice has now officially labelled it “occupational dust exposure”, which sounds far more hardcore than it is.

All of this is swirling around while work at SkyeBridge is making ominous noises about a 2025 restructure and potential voluntary redundancy packages for people at my level. Part of me thinks, “Great, take the money and run, go part‑time somewhere quieter and do more woodworking.” The other part remembers our slightly too‑ambitious Bruntsfield mortgage and the spreadsheets I’ve done assuming a 20–30% income drop.

On top of that, Emma and I are still circling the second‑child conversation. She’s understandably wary – she literally said, “I’m not sure I can handle another baby if we’re also supporting your mum and dad financially,” and with Dad’s recent memory lapses (including getting lost on the way to the Co‑op in North Berwick) and the likely downsizing, it’s hard to argue.

Anyway, if you have any general advice on managing back stuff for people who want to do more hands‑on work without wrecking themselves, I’d really appreciate it. No pressure for a full consult – just “don’t be an idiot, do X/Y/Z” level.

Hope we can all meet somewhere in the middle again soon.

Love,

Jon

metadata.subject: Quick favour re backs + random life update
